e1-week_1.week_focus: AMVF, verification vs. validation, measurement vs. inference, and formal experiment design.

questions.prompt: In the course framing, what is the main job of validation?
options: Checking whether the experiment was performed safely
Checking whether the equations were solved correctly
Checking whether the model is an adequate representation of the real physical system
Checking whether the sensor was calibrated correctly

questions.prompt: Which statement best captures the difference between what a sensor measures and what an engineer often cares about?
options: A sensor always measures the final engineering quantity directly.
A sensor measures a raw signal, and the engineering quantity is often inferred through a calibration or model.
A sensor only measures noise, and the engineer removes it later.
A sensor and a model are unrelated parts of the workflow.

questions.prompt: Which hypothesis is the most testable in the Week 1 sense?
options: “The system will probably change when we run the experiment.”
“We expect interesting behavior.”
“If input voltage is increased from 3 V to 9 V while resistance is held constant, measured power will increase by a factor of nine because power scales with V^2/R.”
“The data should make sense if we do the procedure correctly.”

questions.prompt: Explain the difference between verification and validation in your own words, and give one short engineering-style example of each.
A team says their sensor reads force directly. Based on Week 1, what would you want to ask before accepting that claim?

e1-week_2.week_focus: Variability, measurement error, uncertainty propagation, Monte Carlo reasoning, and correct engineering result statements.

questions.prompt: Which statement best matches the Week 2 distinction?
options: Variability, measurement error, and uncertainty all mean the same thing.
Variability is physical scatter, measurement error is instrument-related deviation, and uncertainty is the quantified bound on what we do not know about the reported result.
Uncertainty is only used when the instrument is broken.
Measurement error disappears if you repeat the measurement enough times.

questions.prompt: Why can two algebraically equivalent power equations produce different uncertainty estimates?
options: Because one of the equations must be physically wrong
Because uncertainty propagation depends on which measured inputs appear in the chosen form and how sensitive the result is to each input
Because engineers are free to choose whichever result they like better
Because uncertainty only applies to nonlinear equations

questions.prompt: Which result is written in the most appropriate Week 2 engineering form?
options: “The answer is 4.98.”
“The power is about 5 watts, probably.”
“P = 4.98 +/- 0.12 W based on partial-derivative propagation.”
“The power should be close to what the meter said.”

questions.prompt: Suppose your Monte Carlo result and your partial-derivative result are close but not identical. Based on Week 2, how should you interpret that?
In the P=IV lab, why might one form of the power equation lead you to care more about improving the current measurement than the resistance measurement?

e1-week_3.week_focus: Validation metrics, calibration vs. validation, multi-sensor comparison, residuals, and critique quality.

questions.prompt: Why is visual overlap on a plot alone not enough to support an engineering comparison claim?
options: Because engineers are not allowed to use plots
Because agreement should be argued using a defined metric and uncertainty-bounded reasoning, not just by eyeballing the figure
Because every plot must have a regression line
Because visual inspection is only useful in chemistry

questions.prompt: Which statement best captures the Week 3 distinction between calibration and validation?
options: Calibration and validation are the same thing with different names
Calibration tests whether a model is predictive, while validation adjusts a sensor to a reference
Calibration is a measurement activity that aligns sensor output to a reference, while validation tests whether a model adequately predicts reality
Validation is only needed if calibration fails

questions.prompt: If two sensors measuring the same quantity disagree outside the accepted comparison bound, what is the best Week 3 conclusion?
options: One sensor must be lying, so delete that dataset immediately
The disagreement is evidence that needs explanation, using uncertainty, sensor behavior, or model limitations
The comparison is irrelevant because two sensors never match perfectly
Validation is complete because two sensors were used

questions.prompt: What does a residual pattern tell you that a single overall error number might not tell you?
If you were critiquing a sample lab report in the Week 3 style, what four things should your critique try to identify?

e1-week_4.week_focus: Reading models to design tests, ranking error sources, sensor selection logic, and integrating the full Phase I pipeline.

questions.prompt: If a model output has to be known within a certain tolerance, what is the most useful Week 4 design move?
options: Start collecting data and hope the uncertainty is small enough
Work backward from the output requirement to determine what measurement quality the inputs must have
Ignore sensor choice because analysis can fix it later
Replace the model with a qualitative description

questions.prompt: Why is ranking dominant error sources useful?
options: It tells you where improvement effort is most likely to matter
It proves the model is valid
It removes the need for uncertainty propagation
It guarantees all sensors will agree

questions.prompt: Which statement best reflects the Week 4 full-pipeline mindset?
options: Experimental design, measurement, uncertainty analysis, and model comparison are separate chores that happen to be in the same report
The report is a single argument from setup to conclusion, and each section depends on the analytical work before it
The most important section is whichever one has the nicest figure
Once data is collected, the AMVF is no longer useful

questions.prompt: Suppose one error term dominates your uncertainty budget by a wide margin. What does Week 4 suggest you should do with that information?
In your own words, what does it mean to say that the Phase I report is a “single integrated argument”?

e1-week_5.week_focus: Prediction before data release, committed reasoning, discrepancy interpretation, and cross-prediction.

questions.prompt: Why does the course require a prediction memo before students see the data?
options: To make the assignment longer
To force students to commit to reasoning before hindsight can reshape the story
To prevent any use of uncertainty analysis
To guarantee that every prediction is correct

questions.prompt: Which of the following is the strongest prediction in the Week 5 sense?
options: “The result will probably be close.”
“The sensor should give reasonable data.”
“The measured response should peak near X with uncertainty about Y, and we will consider agreement acceptable if the data falls within the committed bound.”
“We will know whether the model is good after we look at the data.”

questions.prompt: If a prediction is wrong but the reasoning was careful and committed in advance, what is the best Week 5 interpretation?
options: The work was useless
The student should hide the prediction and rewrite it
The discrepancy is valuable because it can reveal model limitations or missing physics
The experiment automatically failed

questions.prompt: Why is predicting someone else’s experiment from specs a useful training exercise, even though it is not the same as running the experiment yourself?
A team says, “Our prediction was off, but that just means the experiment was bad.” What would Week 5 push you to ask next?

e2-week_1.week_focus: Prediction before exposure, postdiction, intended use, formal response features, and pre-lab commitment.

questions.prompt: Which statement best captures the Week 6 problem with postdiction?
options: It always produces mathematically impossible models
It lets the analyst reshape the explanation after seeing the data, which weakens the credibility of the validation claim
It only happens in biology experiments
It means the experiment should never be repeated

questions.prompt: In Tier 2, why does intended use matter before the experiment runs?
options: It tells you what color to make the plots
It defines what counts as a meaningful prediction and what level of agreement matters for the experiment’s purpose
It replaces the need for a model
It guarantees the calibration is current

questions.prompt: Which item belongs in a defensible pre-lab prediction document?
options: A rewritten explanation after seeing the data
A clear acceptance criterion stated before data collection
A promise to adjust the model until it matches the results
A list of everyone’s favorite plotting styles

questions.prompt: Explain why “the result should look reasonable” is not an adequate Week 6 prediction.
What is the value of naming an anomalous data pattern before the experiment runs?

e2-week_2.week_focus: Sampling, aliasing, signal bandwidth, anti-aliasing filters, and DAQ justification.

questions.prompt: What is aliasing in the Week 7 sense?
options: Random noise caused by a broken sensor
High-frequency signal content folding into a false lower-frequency signal because the sampling rate is too low
A plotting mistake caused by bad axis labels
Any disagreement between a model and experiment

questions.prompt: Signal bandwidth is primarily a property of:
options: The physical phenomenon being measured
The font size on the DAQ screen
The student’s preferred spreadsheet format
The color of the sensor cable

questions.prompt: Why must anti-aliasing happen before the analog-to-digital conversion?
options: Because once aliased data is sampled, the false low-frequency content is already inside the recorded dataset
Because digital filters always increase noise
Because analog components are cheaper than digital ones
Because MATLAB cannot read filtered data

questions.prompt: Why is choosing a sampling rate a modeling commitment rather than just a technical setting?
A dataset shows a clean 120 Hz oscillation. Based on Week 7 alone, why should you still be cautious before concluding the physical system truly contains a 120 Hz mode?

e2-week_3.week_focus: Model adequacy, residual taxonomy, calibration status review, and experimentally informed model updates.

questions.prompt: If residuals show a systematic pattern instead of random scatter, what is the strongest Week 8 inference?
options: The pattern is unimportant if the plot looks nice
The model or setup may be missing a structured physical effect, not just showing random noise
Residuals should always be ignored after calibration
The data must be deleted

questions.prompt: Which statement best captures calibration status review?
options: If the instrument turned on, calibration status is irrelevant
Calibration status review asks whether the instrument’s calibration documentation, interval, and uncertainty support the credibility of the measurement claim
Calibration status review proves the model is valid
Calibration status review replaces uncertainty analysis

questions.prompt: Which is the best example of an experimentally informed model update?
options: “The data was noisy, so we will keep the same model and say the experiment went fine.”
“The residual pattern suggests the model is missing a boundary-condition effect, so the next model revision should explicitly include that effect.”
“We changed the plot color and the model looks better.”
“Calibration and validation are the same, so no update is needed.”

questions.prompt: What is the difference between saying “the model is wrong” and saying “the residual pattern suggests model-form error”?

Residuals increase systematically with load. Name three plausible diagnostic classes, state what evidence would support your preferred explanation, and identify one competing hypothesis you would test.
Why doesn’t a current calibration sticker automatically settle the validation question?

e2-week_4.week_focus: Statistical vs. engineering significance, regression limits, confidence intervals, and data-grounded improvement proposals.

questions.prompt: Which statement best reflects Week 9’s warning about R^2?
options: A high R^2 proves the model is physically correct
A high R^2 means the model explains variance in the fitted data, but it does not by itself prove the model is physically adequate
R^2 is only useful for finance
R^2 replaces residual analysis

questions.prompt: Which statement best captures the difference between statistical significance and engineering significance?
options: They are always identical
Statistical significance concerns whether a difference is distinguishable in the data, while engineering significance concerns whether that difference matters for the intended use or requirement
Engineering significance only matters in civil engineering
Statistical significance only matters when a model fails

questions.prompt: What is the strongest Week 9 reason to use Monte Carlo for a multi-variable system?
options: It avoids having to think about uncertainty sources
It helps propagate multiple uncertain inputs through the model and examine the resulting output distribution, including cases that are awkward for simple closed-form reasoning
It guarantees the prediction will match the experiment
It removes the need to interpret residuals

questions.prompt: A comparison is statistically different, but the gap is still inside the allowed engineering tolerance for the intended use. How should you describe that result?
Why is a data-grounded improvement proposal stronger than a generic suggestion like “use better sensors”?

Two independent configurations have an estimated mean difference of 0.30 N. A Welch 95% confidence interval for the difference is [0.02, 0.58] N, and the pre-specified engineering equivalence region is [−2.0, 2.0] N. What two conclusions should you report?
Which is the correct interpretation of a p-value?
options: The probability that the null hypothesis is true
The probability that the observed difference happened by chance
Assuming the null model and test assumptions are true, the probability of a test statistic at least as extreme as the observed one
The probability that the engineering decision is correct

e2-week_5.week_focus: Quad chart communication, defending analysis choices, peer review quality, and transition from improving existing experiments to designing new ones.

questions.prompt: What is the main purpose of the quad chart presentation in Week 10?
options: To show as many equations as possible on one slide
To communicate the full prediction-to-outcome logic, uncertainty analysis, and improvement argument in a compact, defensible format
To avoid answering questions from the audience
To replace the need for a group report

questions.prompt: What makes a strong peer review comment in the Week 10 sense?
options: “Nice job”
“This was confusing”
A specific comment tied to the technical content, explaining what is correct, what is weak or missing, and what improvement would strengthen the work
A comment that only focuses on slide colors

questions.prompt: Why is E2 a bridge to E3?
options: Because after E2, the course no longer uses models
Because E2 trains students to critique and improve existing experiments, which supports the later task of designing original experiments from scratch
Because E3 is only about presentation style
Because E2 removes the need for prediction

questions.prompt: If someone challenges one of your presentation conclusions during the Q&A, what does Week 10 suggest you should defend first: the conclusion itself, the evidence behind it, or something else?
In one short paragraph, explain how the course’s idea of “good critique” is different from just being negative.

e3-week_1.week_focus: Experimental design vocabulary, hypothesis formulation, variables, confounding, replication, prior art, and standards as design evidence.

questions.prompt: Which statement best captures the Week 11 idea that "experimental design is modeling in hardware"?
options: The experiment should collect as much data as possible before the model is discussed
Every design choice encodes an assumption about what variables matter and what evidence would inform the model
Hardware selection is separate from validation logic
A good design avoids stating a hypothesis until results are available

questions.prompt: A student varies fan voltage during a motor test, but the motor temperature also rises throughout the test and is highest during the high-voltage trials. What is the main experimental design concern?
options: Replication
Confounding
Calibration traceability
Quantization

questions.prompt: Why does Week 11 require ASME V&V 10-2006 and at least one additional relevant source in the preliminary proposal?
options: To make the bibliography longer
To replace the need for a hypothesis
To anchor the design in professional validation language, accepted practices, and prior knowledge relevant to the measurement problem
To prove the proposed experiment will work

questions.prompt: In one short paragraph, distinguish an independent variable, dependent variable, and controlled variable for a possible E3 experiment.
What makes a hypothesis useful for E3 rather than just a vague prediction?

e3-week_2.week_focus: Sensor selection, dynamic range, resolution versus accuracy, mounting and environmental effects, DAQ and signal conditioning choices, and beyond-provided-hardware investigation.

questions.prompt: Which sensor-selection rationale is strongest in the Week 12 sense?
options: "We chose it because it was already in the lab"
"We chose it because it has the most decimal places in the output"
"We chose it because its range, accuracy, bandwidth, mounting requirements, and uncertainty contribution are adequate for the intended validation use"
"We chose it because it is the newest model"

questions.prompt: A sensor reports values to 0.001 units, but its calibrated accuracy is +/-0.2 units. What is the best interpretation?
options: The measurement is accurate to 0.001 units
The display resolution is fine, but the accuracy limit is much larger and must be used in the uncertainty analysis
The sensor has no uncertainty
Resolution and accuracy are the same thing

questions.prompt: Why is a sensor choice incomplete without a DAQ and signal conditioning plan?
options: Because DAQ choices determine whether the sensor signal is sampled, filtered, scaled, and digitized in a way that preserves the information needed for validation
Because DAQ choices only affect file names
Because filtering should always remove as much signal as possible
Because the DAQ replaces the sensor calibration

questions.prompt: Give one example of a mounting or environmental effect that could bias an aerospace measurement and explain why it matters.
What counts as a meaningful "beyond-provided-hardware" investigation for E3?

e3-week_3.week_focus: V&V plan structure, intended use, response features, validation metrics, calibration versus validation, and prospective MATLAB uncertainty analysis.

questions.prompt: In Week 13, why does intended use come before the validation metric?
options: Intended use determines what level and type of agreement is sufficient for the engineering purpose
Intended use is only a writing style choice
Intended use is decided after data collection
Intended use replaces uncertainty analysis

questions.prompt: Which item is a system response feature?
options: "Use MATLAB"
"The maximum deflection of the beam under a specified load"
"Write a clear report"
"Buy a sensor"

questions.prompt: Which statement best distinguishes calibration from validation?
options: Calibration checks an instrument against a known reference; validation checks whether a model predicts system behavior adequately for its intended use
Calibration and validation are identical
Validation is only about sensor zeroing
Calibration proves the model is correct

questions.prompt: What does it mean to use MATLAB uncertainty analysis prospectively in E3?
If prospective uncertainty analysis shows the uncertainty band is too wide to judge the model against the acceptance criterion, what should the design team do?

e3-week_4.week_focus: Oral defense, structured technical critique, peer evaluation, and engineering critique of sample or AI-generated experimental design work.

questions.prompt: What is the main purpose of the Week 14 oral defense?
options: To summarize the report without questions
To defend the experimental design, instrumentation rationale, uncertainty analysis, and validation logic under technical questioning
To replace the V&V plan document
To focus only on slide aesthetics

questions.prompt: Which peer evaluation comment is strongest?
options: "Good job, I liked it"
"The presentation was confusing"
"The proposed sampling rate is not justified relative to the expected signal bandwidth, so the validation plan needs either a bandwidth estimate or a revised sampling/filtering argument"
"The colors were nice"

questions.prompt: Why does Week 14 include engineering critique of sample or AI-generated work?
options: To show that polished writing guarantees technical correctness
To practice applying the same technical standards regardless of whether the work came from a peer, prior report, professional source, or AI system
To avoid evaluating student presentations
To teach prompt engineering as the main course outcome

questions.prompt: During Q&A, someone challenges your validation metric. What should you defend first?
Explain how good critique is different from simply being negative in Week 14.

e3-week_5.week_focus: Course arc integration, AMVF mapping, CLO 6 reflection, experimentally informed models, and the closing question: how do you know this prediction is right?

questions.prompt: Which sequence best describes the course arc?
options: Learn to measure, learn to predict, learn to design
Learn to memorize, learn to copy, learn to avoid uncertainty
Learn to present, learn to present again, learn to present faster
Learn to validate models without experiments

questions.prompt: What is the strongest Week 15 answer to "How do you know this prediction is right?"
options: "Because the model looks reasonable"
"Because the data matched once"

"Because we designed an experiment around the model's intended use, quantified uncertainty, committed to validation metrics before measuring, compared honestly, and used discrepancies to improve the model"
"Because the sensor had many decimal places"

questions.prompt: What is the purpose of the CLO 6 reflection?
options: To list every website the student visited
To describe what method, sensor, platform, or standard the student investigated beyond provided materials, why it mattered, what was learned, and how it affected professional judgment
To prove that the beyond-provided option was always adopted
To replace the technical V&V plan

questions.prompt: In one short paragraph, explain how E1, E2, and E3 each connect to the AMVF.
What does "experimentally informed model" mean by the end of the course?
